summary: I first learned about the attack on the World Trade Center around 9:00 A.M. when a Maytag repairman told me a plane had hit it. Shortly after, another plane hit the other tower, and my brother, who is a Port Authority police officer, and I knew immediately it was an act of terrorism. From about 20 miles away near Edison, I could see smoke where the towers stood, and the city was obscured by ash and smoke. The tragedy deeply affected me, especially knowing friends who worked in the financial area of the World Trade Center and how vulnerable we all felt after the attacks.

My brother is a Port Authority police officer, and on the day of the attacks, he was not called in immediately but came in the next day. The Port Authority Police were actively involved in the response, and there was some confusion about who was in charge at the staging areas like Ellis Island. They were concerned about their personnel and casualties, but no bodies from the Port Authority were brought to Ellis Island. The Port Authority Police were one of the many agencies trying to coordinate during that chaotic time.

At the time of the attacks, I was living in Edison, New Jersey, and after hearing the news, I went home there to get uniforms and food before heading to work. Edison is about 45 minutes from the city, and as I approached, I could see smoke from the World Trade Center. I tried calling work from Edison but couldn't get through because all circuits were busy. It was a pivotal place for me that day as I prepared to respond to the emergency.

I was actually down in Toms River, New Jersey, when the attacks happened, helping my mom move furniture with my brother. After hearing about the attacks, I planned to grab some food and head back to Edison to prepare for work. During that hectic day, I was balancing my responsibilities and the move from Edison to Toms River, which made the long hours and stress even more challenging. Toms River was where I was physically located at the start of the day before heading back toward the city.

When I was driving over the Raritan River bridge on my way home to Edison, I could see the World Trade Center about 10-15 miles away, but that day, the city was completely obscured by smoke. The view was usually clear, but the smoke and ash made it impossible to see the skyline. That moment really brought home the scale of the disaster for me as I approached the city.

I was listening to Howard Stern on the radio while driving toward Edison after the attacks. He was discussing the events as they unfolded, which helped me piece together what was happening. It was surreal hearing the news on his show while seeing the smoke in the distance. Howard Stern's broadcast was one of the sources that kept me informed during the chaos of that morning.

I work at the Statue of Liberty, and on the day of the attacks, the entire island was closed for about three months afterward. Officers working there reported that a plane flew very close to the torch, about 100 feet off, shaking everything, which was terrifying. The Statue and the island became a strategic staging area during the response, and the experience made me realize how vulnerable such iconic places are. The closure and heightened security changed how we viewed our roles and the public's perception of safety.

Ellis Island was designated as a morgue site during the disaster, and when I arrived there around noon, I saw ambulances lined up and tents being set up in anticipation of mass casualties. The New Jersey State Police superintendent later decided Ellis Island was too small for a morgue, so refrigerated trucks were brought to the mainland instead. Ellis Island also served as a staging area for various agencies, and boats continuously transported supplies and personnel there. It was a critical hub in the response effort despite the initial confusion.

The New Jersey State Police played a significant role during the response, including detouring traffic off the turnpike to prevent access toward Manhattan. Their superintendent was involved in decisions about morgue locations, moving the operation from Ellis Island to the mainland. They also used Liberty State Park and Ellis Island as staging areas and coordinated with other agencies. I saw their presence firsthand as they helped manage the chaotic situation and traffic control.

On the day of the attacks, the Holland Tunnel was closed, and there were rumors it was underwater, which added to the confusion. I had to take alternate routes because the tunnel and other access points to Manhattan were shut down. This closure made my commute longer and complicated the response efforts. The Holland Tunnel's closure was part of the broader lockdown of the city’s transportation infrastructure.

Liberty State Park was open during the attacks and served as a major staging area for ambulances and emergency personnel. I saw about 300-400 ambulances lined up along the roads there, ready for mass casualties that thankfully never fully materialized. The park also had facilities like a cafeteria open 24/7 for responders and was used to transport casualties and supplies. It was a key location for coordination among agencies during those first critical weeks.

The Jersey City Police Department was eager to get into Ellis Island and the staging areas, along with other agencies like the New Jersey State Police and Port Authority Police. There was some confusion and competition over who was in charge, which made access control challenging. At one point, we had to close the gate and keep the area secure with officers armed with shotguns. The Jersey City Police were part of the massive interagency response trying to manage the crisis.

At Battery Park, about 200 to 250 casualties were brought over by boats shortly after the attacks. Most of the injured were walking wounded, covered in ash, and only a few needed hospital care. I was at Ellis Island and saw that the casualties were being transported there from Battery Park. It was one of the initial points of arrival for those escaping the disaster at the World Trade Center.

The Salvation Army set up a post at the old railroad terminal near Liberty State Park to support responders and victims. They provided assistance during the days following the attacks, especially after it was clear that Ellis Island would not be used as a morgue. Their presence was part of the broader support network helping manage the crisis and care for those affected. The Salvation Army's role was vital in providing resources and comfort during a very difficult time.

Ground Zero was the epicenter of the disaster, and while I was not directly at the site, I was involved in supporting operations nearby. The ongoing recovery and digging at Ground Zero meant that morgue plans and casualty expectations were constantly evolving. The scale of destruction there was unimaginable, and it shaped much of the response and staging efforts on Ellis Island and Liberty State Park. Ground Zero was the focus of all emergency services during those first weeks.

One of our bigger boats from Jamaica Bay was brought in to help ferry people and supplies between Ellis Island and the mainland. It could carry about 30 people at a time and was running constantly during the first weeks after the attacks. Jamaica Bay's resources were crucial in maintaining the flow of personnel and equipment needed for the response. The boat service helped keep the staging areas connected and operational.

During the attacks, there was talk about Washington, D.C. being a potential target as well. Officers at the Statue of Liberty said a plane flew very close to the torch, and there were other planes in the air, so it was uncertain if other attacks were imminent. The fear extended beyond New York City to places like Washington, D.C., as the nation tried to understand the full scope of the coordinated attacks. It was a tense time with many unknowns about what might happen next.
